meta.certificationName: Microsoft Certified: Azure MLOps Engineer Associate (AI-300)
meta.title: MS-AI300-1: Azure ML Pipelines, Registry and Monitoring

questions.stem: Which of the following machine learning libraries comes pre-installed in the Azure Machine Learning curated environment?

options.text: scikit-learn, MLflow, and XGBoost
scikit-learn, MLflow, and Apache Kafka
MLflow, Apache Flink, and PyTorch
TensorFlow, Apache Hudi, and scikit-learn

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the Azure Machine Learning curated environments pre-install a curated set of ML libraries including scikit-learn, MLflow, XGBoost, LightGBM, TensorFlow, and PyTorch, enabling data scientists to start training without managing dependencies.

**B** is incorrect because Apache Kafka is a streaming platform, not an ML library, and is not bundled in Azure ML curated environments.

**C** is incorrect because Apache Flink is a separate stream-processing engine not included in Azure ML curated environments.

**D** is incorrect because Apache Hudi is a data lake storage format, not an ML library, and is not part of Azure ML curated environments.

[Azure Machine Learning curated environments](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/resource-curated-environments)
questions.reference: [Curated environments - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/resource-curated-environments)

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to track the hyperparameters and evaluation metrics for a model training run using MLflow. Which two function calls should they use?

options.text: mlflow.log_param() for hyperparameters and mlflow.log_metric() for metrics
mlflow.record_param() for hyperparameters and mlflow.record_metric() for metrics
mlflow.log_param() for both hyperparameters and metrics
mlflow.set_tag() for hyperparameters and mlflow.log_metric() for metrics

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because MLflow provides distinct functions: mlflow.log_param() is used for logging scalar hyperparameters (e.g., learning rate, max depth) and mlflow.log_metric() is used for logging numeric evaluation metrics (e.g., accuracy, RMSE) that can be tracked over time with optional steps.

**B** is incorrect because mlflow.record_param() and mlflow.record_metric() are not valid MLflow functions.

**C** is incorrect because mlflow.log_param() is only for parameters, not metrics — log_metric() must be used for numeric evaluation results.

**D** is incorrect because mlflow.set_tag() stores string key-value metadata tags, not numeric hyperparameter values.

[MLflow Tracking API](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-log-view-metrics)
questions.reference: [Log metrics, parameters, and files with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-log-view-metrics)

questions.stem:
A data scientist runs the following code:

```python
import mlflow
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier
from sklearn.datasets import load_iris
from sklearn.model_selection import train_test_split

X, y = load_iris(return_X_y=True)
X_train, X_test, y_train, y_test = train_test_split(X, y, random_state=42)

mlflow.sklearn.autolog()

with mlflow.start_run():
    clf = RandomForestClassifier(n_estimators=100, max_depth=5)
    clf.fit(X_train, y_train)
```

Which items are automatically logged by MLflow autologging for sklearn?

options.text: Model parameters (n_estimators, max_depth), training metrics, and the fitted model as an artifact
Model parameters only; metrics and artifacts must be logged manually
The fitted model artifact only; parameters and metrics must be logged manually
Model parameters, training metrics, the fitted model, and the raw training dataset

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because mlflow.sklearn.autolog() automatically captures estimator parameters (such as n_estimators and max_depth), training metrics (such as training accuracy), and saves the fitted model as a logged artifact.

**B** is incorrect because autolog captures more than just parameters — it also logs training metrics and the trained model artifact.

**C** is incorrect because autolog captures parameters and metrics, not just the model artifact.

**D** is incorrect because autolog does NOT automatically log the raw training dataset; that must be done manually with mlflow.log_artifact() or the MLflow datasets API.

[MLflow autologging for scikit-learn in Azure ML](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-use-mlflow-configure-tracking)
questions.reference: [Configure MLflow for Azure Machine Learning - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-use-mlflow-configure-tracking)

questions.stem: An ML engineer uses Azure Machine Learning automated ML to build a classification model. Which of the following statements correctly describes what AutoML produces upon completion?

options.text: A summary of trial runs, the best model registered in the Azure ML model registry, and an editable notebook with the best run's code
A single production-ready model deployed behind a REST endpoint with no further action needed
A summary of trial runs and the best model artifact; no notebook is generated
A set of feature engineering recommendations but no trained models

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Azure ML automated ML runs multiple trials, logs each run to MLflow, can register the best model in the Azure ML model registry, and generates a source notebook containing the full pipeline code for the winning run so the data scientist can inspect and customize it.

**B** is incorrect because AutoML does not automatically deploy a REST endpoint; deployment is a separate step that requires explicit configuration.

**C** is incorrect because AutoML does generate an editable notebook for the best run to enable customization and reproducibility.

**D** is incorrect because AutoML actually trains and evaluates multiple models; it does not only make feature engineering recommendations.

[Azure Machine Learning automated ML](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-automated-ml)
questions.reference: [What is automated ML? AutoML - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-automated-ml)

questions.stem: Which statement best reflects the recommended way to identify promoted models in the current Azure Machine Learning registry experience?

options.text: Use model aliases such as `champion` or `candidate` to point to the appropriate model version
Rely on the fixed MLflow stage sequence None → Staging → Production → Archived for all registry workflows
Mark promotion state only by renaming the registered model itself
Use compute cluster tags instead of model metadata or aliases

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because the current Azure Machine Learning registry guidance emphasizes **model aliases** to identify promoted versions such as a champion model without depending on deprecated stage transitions. **B** is incorrect because MLflow model stages are deprecated for newer registry workflows. **C** is incorrect because renaming the model object is not how version promotion is managed. **D** is incorrect because compute tags do not provide model-version lifecycle control.
questions.reference: [Manage models, registries, and aliases in Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-manage-models)

questions.stem: A data scientist logs a file called `confusion_matrix.png` during a training run. Which MLflow call is correct?

options.text: mlflow.log_artifact('confusion_matrix.png')
mlflow.log_metric('confusion_matrix.png')
mlflow.log_param('confusion_matrix', 'confusion_matrix.png')
mlflow.log_model('confusion_matrix.png', 'image')

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because mlflow.log_artifact() is used to save local files (images, plots, CSVs, etc.) to the run's artifact store, making them available for review in the Azure ML experiment UI.

**B** is incorrect because mlflow.log_metric() is for numeric scalar values, not file paths.

**C** is incorrect because mlflow.log_param() stores string key-value pairs describing hyperparameters, not files.

**D** is incorrect because mlflow.log_model() is for serializing and logging a trained ML model object, not an image file.

[MLflow artifacts in Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-log-view-metrics)
questions.reference: [Log metrics, parameters, and files with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-log-view-metrics)

questions.stem: A team wants to use Azure Machine Learning automated ML for a regression task. Select TWO limitations that apply to Azure Machine Learning automated ML. (Select TWO)

options.text: AutoML cannot perform time-series forecasting
AutoML does not support custom loss functions
AutoML only supports structured (tabular) data, not unstructured data like raw text or images natively
AutoML cannot log runs to MLflow

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Azure ML automated ML uses a fixed set of algorithms and evaluation metrics; users cannot inject custom loss functions into AutoML trials.

**C** is correct because AutoML is designed for structured tabular data and does not natively handle unstructured inputs such as raw image pixels or free-form text without prior feature extraction.

**A** is incorrect because Azure ML automated ML does support time-series forecasting as a task type.

**D** is incorrect because AutoML automatically logs all trial runs to MLflow and can register the best model in the model registry.

[Azure Machine Learning automated ML limitations](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-automated-ml)
questions.reference: [What is automated ML? AutoML - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-automated-ml)

questions.stem: What is the primary difference between the standard Azure Machine Learning curated environment and the GPU-enabled curated environment?

options.text: The GPU-enabled environment includes CUDA libraries and GPU-optimized builds of deep learning frameworks like TensorFlow and PyTorch
The GPU-enabled environment includes additional SQL analytics libraries not present in the standard environment
The GPU-enabled environment removes MLflow and replaces it with TensorBoard
The GPU-enabled environment is identical to the standard environment but runs on a faster CPU

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the GPU-enabled Azure ML curated environment is configured with NVIDIA CUDA, cuDNN, and GPU-optimized builds of deep learning frameworks (TensorFlow, PyTorch), enabling GPU-accelerated training on NC-series or ND-series compute.

**B** is incorrect because the GPU environment is optimized for deep learning, not SQL analytics workloads.

**C** is incorrect because MLflow is present in both environments; TensorBoard does not replace it.

**D** is incorrect because GPU environments use actual GPU instances, not simply faster CPUs — GPUs provide massively parallel compute for matrix operations.

[Azure Machine Learning GPU curated environments](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/resource-curated-environments)
questions.reference: [Curated environments - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/resource-curated-environments)

questions.stem:
An ML engineer registers a newly trained model in the Azure Machine Learning model registry with the following code:

```python
mlflow.sklearn.log_model(
    sk_model=model,
    artifact_path='model',
    registered_model_name='churn_predictor'
)
```

What stage will this model version be in immediately after registration?

options.text: None
Staging
Production
Development

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because when a model is first registered in the Azure ML model registry (whether via log_model with registered_model_name or mlflow.register_model()), its initial stage is 'None'. The engineer must explicitly transition it to Staging or Production using MlflowClient().transition_model_version_stage().

**B** is incorrect because Staging must be explicitly set after registration — it is not assigned automatically.

**C** is incorrect because Production must be explicitly set and is not assigned automatically on registration.

**D** is incorrect because Development is not a valid stage in the Azure ML model registry.

[Azure Machine Learning model registry stages](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-model-management-and-deployment)
questions.reference: [MLOps machine learning model management - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-model-management-and-deployment)

questions.stem: A data scientist enables autologging with mlflow.autolog() before training a scikit-learn model. Select TWO items that are NOT automatically logged by sklearn autologging. (Select TWO)

options.text: Model hyperparameters (e.g., C, max_iter)
Custom business metrics computed after prediction (e.g., revenue impact)
Training dataset schema and raw data
Test set evaluation metrics computed via model.score()

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because custom business metrics calculated outside the model training loop (such as revenue impact from predictions) are not captured by autologging; they require explicit mlflow.log_metric() calls.

**C** is correct because sklearn autologging does not log the raw training dataset or its schema; only model parameters and built-in training metrics are captured automatically.

**A** is incorrect because model hyperparameters are automatically captured by sklearn autologging.

**D** is incorrect because test set metrics are logged when the user calls model.score() or cross_val_score() inside an active autolog context.

[MLflow autologging for scikit-learn](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-use-mlflow-configure-tracking)
questions.reference: [Configure MLflow for Azure Machine Learning - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-use-mlflow-configure-tracking)

questions.stem: A data scientist has a dataset with a highly imbalanced binary target (95% negative, 5% positive). Which evaluation metric is MOST appropriate for assessing model performance on the positive class?

options.text: F1 score
Accuracy
Mean Squared Error
R-squared

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because F1 score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall, making it sensitive to performance on the minority positive class. It is well-suited for imbalanced datasets where simply predicting the majority class would yield misleadingly high accuracy.

**B** is incorrect because a model that predicts all negatives would achieve 95% accuracy without ever correctly identifying a positive case — accuracy is misleading on imbalanced datasets.

**C** is incorrect because Mean Squared Error is a regression metric, not a classification metric.

**D** is incorrect because R-squared is also a regression metric used for continuous target variables.

[Evaluation metrics for imbalanced classification](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-understand-automated-ml)
questions.reference: [Evaluate AutoML experiment results - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-understand-automated-ml)

questions.stem: A data scientist needs to create a train/test split while ensuring that the proportion of each class label is preserved in both sets. Which sklearn function and parameter achieves this?

options.text: train_test_split(X, y, stratify=y)
train_test_split(X, y, shuffle=False)
train_test_split(X, y, random_state=42)
train_test_split(X, y, test_size=0.2, balanced=True)

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the stratify parameter in train_test_split accepts the target array y and ensures that each split preserves the same class distribution as the original dataset, which is critical for imbalanced problems.

**B** is incorrect because shuffle=False disables shuffling before splitting, which is useful for time series data but does not control class proportions.

**C** is incorrect because random_state only controls reproducibility of the random split, not class distribution across splits.

**D** is incorrect because balanced=True is not a valid parameter for train_test_split — it does not exist in sklearn.

[Stratified train/test split in sklearn](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-train-scikit-learn)
questions.reference: [Train scikit-learn machine learning models (v2) - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-train-scikit-learn)

questions.stem: A data scientist is using GridSearchCV on a pipeline with StandardScaler and LogisticRegression. The data has 10,000 rows and cv=5. Why is it important to include StandardScaler inside the pipeline rather than fitting it on the full dataset before GridSearchCV?

options.text: Fitting StandardScaler before cross-validation causes data leakage because the scaler learns statistics from validation fold data
StandardScaler cannot be used with GridSearchCV
Including StandardScaler in the pipeline makes cross-validation run faster
StandardScaler must be fit separately before being passed to a pipeline

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because if StandardScaler is fit on the entire dataset before cross-validation, the scaler learns mean and variance from validation fold data that the model should not have seen during training. This constitutes data leakage, leading to overly optimistic performance estimates. By placing StandardScaler inside the pipeline, GridSearchCV ensures the scaler is fit only on training folds during each CV iteration.

**B** is incorrect because StandardScaler works perfectly within sklearn pipelines and GridSearchCV — it is one of the primary use cases.

**C** is incorrect because the pipeline does not speed up cross-validation; it actually adds overhead due to refitting the scaler on each fold.

**D** is incorrect because StandardScaler is designed to be used inside pipelines and does not need to be pre-fit before being added.

[Avoiding data leakage in cross-validation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-train-scikit-learn)
questions.reference: [Train scikit-learn machine learning models (v2) - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-train-scikit-learn)

questions.stem: A data scientist is building a churn prediction model and wants to encode a high-cardinality categorical feature (e.g., zip code with 1,000 unique values). Which encoding strategy is most appropriate?

options.text: Target encoding or hashing, to avoid creating 1,000 binary columns
OneHotEncoder, to create one binary column per unique value
OrdinalEncoder assigning integers 0–999 and treating them as numeric
No encoding is needed; tree models handle categorical strings natively in sklearn

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because target encoding summarizes a categorical feature with target-related statistics (e.g., mean of the target per category), and hashing encodes values into a fixed-size vector, both of which handle high-cardinality features without dimensional explosion.

**B** is incorrect because OneHotEncoding 1,000 unique zip codes would create 1,000 binary columns, causing the curse of dimensionality and memory issues.

**C** is incorrect because assigning arbitrary ordinal integers to nominal categories like zip codes implies a false ordering that distance-based models and linear models may misinterpret.

**D** is incorrect because scikit-learn estimators do not natively handle string categoricals; encoding is required before fitting.

[Encoding high-cardinality features in Azure ML](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-train-scikit-learn)
questions.reference: [Train scikit-learn machine learning models (v2) - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-train-scikit-learn)

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to start an MLflow tracking run and log a hyperparameter called `learning_rate` with value 0.01. Which code is correct?

options.text:
with mlflow.start_run():
    mlflow.log_param('learning_rate', 0.01)

with mlflow.start_run():
    mlflow.log_metric('learning_rate', 0.01)
mlflow.set_param('learning_rate', 0.01)
mlflow.track(param='learning_rate', value=0.01)

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because mlflow.log_param() is the correct function for logging a single key-value parameter within an active run context opened by mlflow.start_run().

**B** is incorrect because mlflow.log_metric() is for numeric evaluation metrics (which can be tracked over time with steps), not hyperparameters.

**C** is incorrect because mlflow.set_param() is not a valid MLflow function.

**D** is incorrect because mlflow.track() is not a valid MLflow function.

[MLflow log_param in Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-log-view-metrics)
questions.reference: [Log metrics, parameters, and files with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-log-view-metrics)

questions.stem: Which Azure Machine Learning automated ML task type should a data scientist use to build a binary classification model predicting customer churn?

options.text: Regression
Classification
Forecasting
Clustering

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Classification is the AutoML task type for building models that predict discrete class labels, including binary classification tasks like churn prediction (churn / no churn).

**A** is incorrect because Regression is for tasks where the target is a continuous numeric value, not a discrete class label.

**C** is incorrect because Forecasting is for time-series prediction tasks where the goal is to predict future numeric values.

**D** is incorrect because Clustering is an unsupervised learning task not supported by Azure ML AutoML — AutoML only handles supervised learning tasks.

[Azure Machine Learning automated ML task types](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-automated-ml)
questions.reference: [What is automated ML? AutoML - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-automated-ml)

questions.stem: An ML engineer wants to register an existing MLflow run's model in the Azure ML model registry. The run ID is `abc123` and the model was logged to the path `model`. Which code achieves this?

options.text: mlflow.register_model('runs:/abc123/model', 'my_model')
mlflow.log_model(run_id='abc123', registered_model_name='my_model')
mlflow.Model.register(run_id='abc123', path='model')
mlflow.tracking.register('runs:/abc123/model')

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because mlflow.register_model() takes a model URI in the form 'runs:/<run_id>/<artifact_path>' and a registered model name, creating a new version in the model registry linked to that run.

**B** is incorrect because mlflow.log_model() is used during training to log a model object; it does not register an already-logged model from a past run.

**C** is incorrect because mlflow.Model.register() is not a valid MLflow function.

**D** is incorrect because mlflow.tracking.register() is not a valid MLflow function.

[mlflow.register_model in Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-manage-models-mlflow)
questions.reference: [Manage models registry with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-manage-models-mlflow)

questions.stem: A data scientist uses mlflow.log_metric('val_loss', loss_value, step=epoch) inside a training loop. What is the purpose of the `step` parameter?

options.text: It associates the metric value with a specific training step or epoch, enabling the MLflow UI to display the metric as a time-series plot
It sets the learning rate step size for gradient descent
It specifies how many decimal places to round the metric value to
It is required for all mlflow.log_metric() calls; omitting it raises an error

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the optional step parameter in mlflow.log_metric() is an integer that allows the same metric key to be logged multiple times at different steps (e.g., epochs), creating a time-series that can be visualized as a chart in the MLflow UI within Azure ML.

**B** is incorrect because the step parameter has no relationship to gradient descent learning rate — it is purely a logging index.

**C** is incorrect because MLflow does not round metric values based on a step parameter.

**D** is incorrect because the step parameter is optional; omitting it defaults to step=0 and the metric is still logged without error.

[mlflow.log_metric step parameter](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-log-view-metrics)
questions.reference: [Log metrics, parameters, and files with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-log-view-metrics)

questions.stem: An ML team wants to use the Azure ML model registry to manage model lifecycle. Select TWO valid operations the model registry supports. (Select TWO)

options.text: Transitioning a model version from Staging to Production
Comparing metrics across different versions of a registered model
Automatically retraining a model when new data arrives
Serving the model as a REST API with zero configuration

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because one of the core model registry features is the ability to transition model versions through lifecycle stages (None → Staging → Production → Archived) to manage deployment promotion.

**B** is correct because the MLflow UI and API allow users to view and compare metrics, parameters, and artifacts logged across different versions of a registered model.

**C** is incorrect because the model registry does not trigger or manage automated retraining; that requires external orchestration (e.g., Azure ML pipelines, Azure DevOps).

**D** is incorrect because while Azure ML online endpoints can deploy registered models, the model registry itself does not automatically expose a REST API with zero configuration.

[Azure Machine Learning model registry](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-model-management-and-deployment)
questions.reference: [MLOps machine learning model management - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-model-management-and-deployment)

questions.stem: A data scientist sets up autologging with `mlflow.autolog()` before training a scikit-learn Pipeline that includes a StandardScaler and a LogisticRegression. Which of the following is TRUE about what gets logged?

options.text: All pipeline stage parameters (including StandardScaler's with_mean, LogisticRegression's C and max_iter) and training metrics are logged automatically
Only the final estimator's parameters (LogisticRegression) are logged; preprocessor parameters are ignored
mlflow.autolog() does not work with sklearn Pipelines; each stage must be logged manually
Only metrics are logged; all parameters must be logged with mlflow.log_param()

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because sklearn autologging is pipeline-aware: it logs parameters from all stages in the pipeline (using the stage_name__param_name convention), not just the final estimator. Training metrics are also captured automatically.

**B** is incorrect because sklearn autologging extracts and logs parameters from all named steps in the pipeline, including preprocessors like StandardScaler.

**C** is incorrect because sklearn autologging fully supports Pipeline objects; it is one of the primary use cases for the feature.

**D** is incorrect because autologging captures both parameters and metrics automatically — no manual log_param() calls are needed for pipeline parameters.

[MLflow autologging with sklearn Pipelines](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-use-mlflow-configure-tracking)
questions.reference: [Configure MLflow for Azure Machine Learning - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-use-mlflow-configure-tracking)

questions.stem: An ML engineer needs to configure an Azure Machine Learning compute cluster for training a large PyTorch model that requires GPU acceleration. Which cluster configuration is MOST appropriate?

options.text: Use the GPU-enabled curated environment on a compute cluster with GPU instance types (e.g., NC-series or ND-series on Azure)
Use the standard CPU curated environment on a cluster with the maximum number of CPU cores
Use the standard curated environment and install PyTorch manually via pip on the cluster
Use a standard CPU instance; GPUs are not supported in Azure Machine Learning compute clusters

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because the GPU-enabled Azure ML curated environment includes CUDA libraries and GPU-optimized builds of PyTorch, and must be paired with GPU-enabled instance types (such as NC-series or ND-series on Azure) for GPU-accelerated training.

**B** is incorrect because a CPU cluster cannot perform GPU training regardless of the environment used — the underlying hardware must have GPUs.

**C** is incorrect because manually installing PyTorch on a standard environment is possible but creates CUDA compatibility risks; the GPU-enabled curated environment is purpose-built and tested for this use case.

**D** is incorrect because Azure Machine Learning fully supports GPU compute clusters; NC-series and ND-series VM sizes are explicitly designed for GPU training workloads.

[Azure Machine Learning GPU compute clusters](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-create-attach-compute-cluster)
questions.reference: [Create compute clusters - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-create-attach-compute-cluster)

questions.stem: A data scientist uses Azure Machine Learning automated ML to run a classification experiment. After the experiment completes, where can they find the best model?

options.text: In a dedicated AutoML dashboard separate from the Azure ML studio experiment view
In the MLflow experiment linked to the AutoML run, where the best trial is identified by the primary metric
In Azure Blob Storage as a serialized pickle file with no metadata
The best model must be manually identified by inspecting all trial logs

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because Azure ML automated ML logs all trials to an MLflow experiment and clearly identifies the best run based on the primary metric. The returned AutoML job object also provides programmatic access to the best run.

**A** is incorrect because AutoML uses the standard Azure ML studio experiment and MLflow UI; there is no separate dedicated AutoML dashboard.

**C** is incorrect because AutoML does not save models as raw pickle files in Blob Storage; models are logged to MLflow runs with full metadata.

**D** is incorrect because AutoML automatically identifies and surfaces the best trial; manual inspection is not required.

[Azure Machine Learning automated ML results](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-configure-auto-train)
questions.reference: [Set up AutoML with Python (v2) - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-configure-auto-train)

questions.stem: A model version is currently referenced by the `champion` alias in Azure Machine Learning. An engineer wants to retire it because a newer version has passed validation. What should they do?

options.text: Move the `champion` alias to the newer approved model version and remove or repurpose the old alias as needed
Change the model version stage from Production to Archived because aliases cannot be updated
Delete the entire registered model so the alias is cleared automatically
Retag the training compute cluster because aliases are inherited from compute

questions.explanation: **A** is correct because Azure Machine Learning supports using **aliases** to reference the active model version; promoting a new version typically means updating the alias to point at that version. **B** is incorrect because stage-based promotion is not the recommended current pattern. **C** is incorrect because retiring one version does not require deleting the whole registered model. **D** is incorrect because compute resources do not control model aliases.
questions.reference: [Manage models, registries, and aliases in Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-manage-models)

questions.stem: A data scientist is comparing Azure Machine Learning automated ML to manually building a model. Select TWO TRUE statements about Azure Machine Learning automated ML. (Select TWO)

options.text: AutoML explores multiple algorithms and hyperparameter combinations, logging each as a separate MLflow run
AutoML generates a source notebook for the best run that can be cloned and customized
AutoML supports custom neural network architectures defined in PyTorch
AutoML automatically handles feature engineering for all data types including unstructured text and images

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because Azure ML automated ML systematically explores a configurable set of algorithms (e.g., LightGBM, scikit-learn models, XGBoost) and hyperparameter combinations, logging each trial as a separate MLflow run for full observability.

**B** is correct because at the end of an AutoML experiment, Azure ML generates an editable Python notebook containing the full code for the best run, which data scientists can clone, inspect, and customize.

**C** is incorrect because AutoML does not support custom neural network architectures; it uses a fixed set of supported classical ML and ensemble algorithms.

**D** is incorrect because AutoML is designed for structured tabular data and does not perform feature engineering on unstructured data such as raw images or free-form text.

[Azure Machine Learning automated ML features](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-automated-ml)
questions.reference: [What is automated ML? AutoML - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/concept-automated-ml)

questions.stem: A data scientist needs to scale numeric features to have zero mean and unit variance before training a Support Vector Machine. Which sklearn transformer should they use?

options.text: MinMaxScaler
StandardScaler
LabelEncoder
Normalizer

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because StandardScaler standardizes features by subtracting the mean and dividing by the standard deviation, resulting in features with zero mean and unit variance — the canonical preprocessing for SVMs and other distance-based algorithms.

**A** is incorrect because MinMaxScaler scales features to a fixed range (default [0, 1]) but does not produce zero mean and unit variance.

**C** is incorrect because LabelEncoder converts class labels to integer indices; it is used for target encoding, not feature scaling.

**D** is incorrect because Normalizer scales each sample (row) to unit norm, not each feature (column) to zero mean and unit variance.

[sklearn StandardScaler documentation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-train-scikit-learn)
questions.reference: [Train scikit-learn machine learning models (v2) - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-train-scikit-learn)

questions.stem: A data scientist is building a time-series model to predict daily sales. They need to create train and test sets. Why should they NOT use random shuffling when splitting this dataset?

options.text: Random shuffling breaks the temporal order, causing future data to leak into the training set, which inflates performance estimates
Random shuffling causes class imbalance in time-series data
sklearn's train_test_split does not support time-series data
Random shuffling reduces the size of the training set

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because time-series data has a temporal dependency structure: future values are correlated with past values. Random shuffling mixes future observations into the training set, allowing the model to learn from data it would not have access to in production. The correct approach is a chronological split.

**B** is incorrect because class imbalance is a separate issue unrelated to the temporal ordering problem in time-series splitting.

**C** is incorrect because sklearn's train_test_split works with any array; however, it must be called with shuffle=False for time-series data to maintain chronological order.

**D** is incorrect because the size of the train/test split is controlled by the test_size parameter, not by whether shuffling is used.

[Time-series splitting best practices](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-configure-auto-train)
questions.reference: [Set up AutoML with Python (v2) - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-configure-auto-train)

questions.stem: A data scientist trains a model with 5-fold cross-validation using cross_val_score. The scores are [0.82, 0.85, 0.79, 0.88, 0.84]. What is the best interpretation of these results?

options.text: The model has an estimated generalization accuracy of approximately 0.836 ± some variance, providing a more robust estimate than a single train/test split
The model will achieve exactly 83.6% accuracy on new data
The best fold score of 0.88 should be used as the model's performance estimate
The model fails cross-validation because the scores vary across folds

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because k-fold cross-validation trains and evaluates the model k times on different data subsets, producing k performance estimates whose mean (here approximately 0.836) and standard deviation provide a more statistically robust estimate of generalization performance than a single split.

**B** is incorrect because cross-validation provides an estimate with uncertainty; exact performance on unseen data cannot be guaranteed.

**C** is incorrect because cherry-picking the best fold overstates performance; the mean across all folds is the appropriate estimate.

**D** is incorrect because variation across folds is expected and normal — it is informative about model stability, not a sign of failure.

[Cross-validation with cross_val_score](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-train-scikit-learn)
questions.reference: [Train scikit-learn machine learning models (v2) - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-train-scikit-learn)

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to track all parameters and metrics for a series of model training runs under a shared project namespace. Which MLflow function should they call first to group these runs together?

options.text: mlflow.start_run()
mlflow.set_experiment()
mlflow.create_experiment()
mlflow.set_tracking_uri()

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because mlflow.set_experiment() sets the active experiment by name, creating it if it does not exist, and all subsequent runs belong to that experiment.

**A** is incorrect because start_run() begins a run but does not define the experiment grouping — runs go to the default experiment unless set_experiment() is called first.

**C** is incorrect because create_experiment() only creates the experiment but does not set it as active for subsequent logging calls.

**D** is incorrect because set_tracking_uri() configures where the MLflow tracking server is located, not the experiment grouping.

[MLflow experiments — mlflow.set_experiment()](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-use-mlflow-configure-tracking)
questions.reference: [Configure MLflow for Azure Machine Learning - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-use-mlflow-configure-tracking)

questions.stem: Which MLflow function is used to log a scalar metric value (such as validation RMSE) during a training run?

options.text: mlflow.log_param()
mlflow.log_artifact()
mlflow.log_metric()
mlflow.set_tag()

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because mlflow.log_metric() is specifically for logging numeric scalar values (e.g., accuracy, RMSE) that can be plotted over steps in the Azure ML experiment UI.

**A** is incorrect because log_param() logs a hyperparameter string or number but is meant for configuration values, not evaluation metrics tracked over time.

**B** is incorrect because log_artifact() logs a file or directory as an artifact, not a scalar numeric value.

**D** is incorrect because set_tag() stores free-text metadata tags on a run, not numeric metrics.

[MLflow Tracking — mlflow.log_metric()](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-log-view-metrics)
questions.reference: [Log metrics, parameters, and files with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-log-view-metrics)

questions.stem: A data scientist enables MLflow autologging before training a scikit-learn RandomForestClassifier. Which of the following artifacts is NOT automatically logged by mlflow.sklearn.autolog()?

options.text: Model parameters (e.g., n_estimators, max_depth)
Training metrics (e.g., training accuracy)
The trained model as an MLflow artifact
Custom business KPIs defined outside the training function

questions.explanation:
**D** is correct because autologging only captures what the sklearn framework itself produces — parameters, standard metrics, and the trained model. Custom business KPIs computed outside the sklearn training loop (e.g., revenue impact, SLA compliance) must be logged manually with mlflow.log_metric().

**A** is incorrect because model parameters are automatically captured by mlflow.sklearn.autolog().

**B** is incorrect because training metrics are automatically captured by mlflow.sklearn.autolog().

**C** is incorrect because the trained model artifact is automatically saved by mlflow.sklearn.autolog().

[MLflow autologging — mlflow.sklearn.autolog()](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-use-mlflow-configure-tracking)
questions.reference: [Configure MLflow for Azure Machine Learning - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-use-mlflow-configure-tracking)

questions.stem: An ML engineer needs to run a large-scale hyperparameter search across 200 combinations using HyperOpt. They want the trials to be distributed across a Spark cluster. Which object should they pass to fmin() to enable distributed execution?

options.text: Trials()
SparkTrials(parallelism=8)
mlflow.start_run(nested=True)
SparkContext.parallelize()

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because SparkTrials is the HyperOpt trials object designed for distributed execution — it distributes each trial as a Spark task and allows the parallelism argument to control concurrency across the cluster.

**A** is incorrect because Trials() runs trials sequentially on the driver node, not distributed across a cluster.

**C** is incorrect because start_run(nested=True) creates a child run in MLflow for tracking but is unrelated to distributing HyperOpt trials.

**D** is incorrect because SparkContext.parallelize() is a general Spark RDD operation and has no direct HyperOpt integration.

[HyperOpt SparkTrials for distributed tuning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-tune-hyperparameters)
questions.reference: [Hyperparameter tuning a model (v2) - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-tune-hyperparameters)

questions.stem: A data scientist wants to record a SHAP summary plot as part of an MLflow run. After generating the matplotlib figure object `fig`, which call correctly logs it to the active run?

options.text: mlflow.log_metric('shap_plot', fig)
mlflow.log_artifact(fig)
mlflow.log_figure(fig, 'shap_summary.png')
mlflow.log_param('shap_plot', fig)

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because mlflow.log_figure() accepts a matplotlib or plotly figure object and a destination filename, saving it as an artifact in the run without requiring the caller to save the file to disk first.

**A** is incorrect because log_metric() only accepts numeric scalars, not figure objects.

**B** is incorrect because log_artifact() requires a file path string, not a figure object directly.

**D** is incorrect because log_param() stores string or numeric hyperparameters, not figures.

[MLflow Tracking — mlflow.log_figure()](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-log-view-metrics)
questions.reference: [Log metrics, parameters, and files with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-log-view-metrics)

questions.stem: An ML engineer is running a hyperparameter sweep where each combination spawns its own MLflow run, but all should be grouped under a single parent run for easy comparison. What is the correct pattern?

options.text: Call mlflow.start_run() once for all child runs and pass nested=False to each
Use with mlflow.start_run() as parent, then call mlflow.start_run(nested=True) for each child trial
Set the same run_name for all runs using mlflow.set_tag('run_name', parent_id)
Log all child metrics to the parent run using mlflow.log_metrics() with a prefix

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the standard MLflow pattern for hyperparameter sweeps is to open a parent run with mlflow.start_run() and then open each child trial with mlflow.start_run(nested=True) inside a loop. This creates the parent-child run hierarchy visible in the Azure ML experiment UI.

**A** is incorrect because nested=False would not create a child relationship — the runs would be siblings, not children of the parent.

**C** is incorrect because run_name is just metadata and does not establish a parent-child hierarchy in the MLflow run tree.

**D** is incorrect because logging all metrics to the parent run loses the per-trial granularity that makes hyperparameter comparison meaningful.

[MLflow parent/child run relationships with nested=True](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-use-mlflow-configure-tracking)
questions.reference: [Configure MLflow for Azure Machine Learning - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-use-mlflow-configure-tracking)

questions.stem: A team is comparing two models logged in MLflow. Model A has val_auc=0.87 logged in run abc123, and Model B has val_auc=0.91 in run def456. Using the MlflowClient, which method would return a list of runs for programmatic comparison?

options.text: MlflowClient().get_run(run_id)
MlflowClient().search_runs(experiment_ids=['1'], filter_string='metrics.val_auc > 0.85')
mlflow.list_artifacts(run_id)
mlflow.get_experiment_by_name(experiment_name)

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because MlflowClient().search_runs() returns a list of run objects filtered by metric thresholds, params, or tags, enabling programmatic comparison across multiple runs in an experiment.

**A** is incorrect because get_run() retrieves a single run by ID — not useful for multi-run comparison.

**C** is incorrect because list_artifacts() returns file artifacts for a single run, not metric comparisons across runs.

**D** is incorrect because get_experiment_by_name() returns experiment metadata but not the runs within it.

[MlflowClient — search_runs() for multi-run comparison](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-mlflow)
questions.reference: [Query & compare experiments and runs with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-track-experiments-mlflow)

questions.stem: A data scientist uses mlflow.models.infer_signature() to record a model signature. Select TWO reasons why recording a model signature is important for production deployments. (Select TWO)

options.text: It enforces input/output schema validation at serving time, preventing silent type mismatches
It automatically retrains the model when input distributions shift
It enables Azure Machine Learning online endpoints to generate accurate API documentation for the endpoint
It compresses the model artifact to reduce storage costs

questions.explanation:
**A** is correct because a model signature defines the expected input columns and types; MLflow serving enforces this schema and rejects malformed requests at inference time, preventing silent data type mismatches.

**C** is correct because Azure ML online endpoints use the signature to auto-generate API documentation showing expected input and output shapes in the deployment UI and REST schema.

**B** is incorrect because signatures do not trigger retraining — that is the responsibility of monitoring and alerting pipelines.

**D** is incorrect because signatures are metadata stored alongside the model and have no effect on model file size or compression.

[MLflow model signatures](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-manage-models-mlflow)
questions.reference: [Manage models registry with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-manage-models-mlflow)

questions.stem: Which HyperOpt search space expression should a data scientist use to define a continuous uniform distribution between 0.001 and 0.1 for a learning rate hyperparameter?

options.text: hp.uniform('lr', 0.001, 0.1)
hp.randint('lr', 100)

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because hp.uniform('lr', low, high) samples floating-point values uniformly between low and high, which is the standard choice for continuous hyperparameters like learning rate.

**A** is incorrect because hp.choice() picks from a discrete list — correct if you only want those three values, but not a continuous distribution.

**C** is incorrect because hp.randint() samples integers from 0 to n, not floats in a continuous range.

**D** is incorrect because hp.quniform() samples at discrete intervals (quantized uniform), not a truly continuous distribution.

[HyperOpt hp.uniform() search space](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-tune-hyperparameters)
questions.reference: [Hyperparameter tuning a model (v2) - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-tune-hyperparameters)

questions.stem: An ML engineer has trained a model and wants to register it in the Azure Machine Learning model registry so it can be promoted through stages. Which function registers a logged model run artifact to the registry?

options.text: mlflow.log_model(model, 'my_model')
mlflow.register_model('runs:/abc123/my_model', 'ProductionModel')
MlflowClient().create_registered_model('ProductionModel')
mlflow.save_model(model, '/tmp/my_model')

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because mlflow.register_model() takes a run artifact URI (runs:/<run_id>/<artifact_path>) and a registry model name, creating a new version in the model registry linked to that run.

**A** is incorrect because log_model() saves the model artifact to the current run but does not register it in the model registry.

**C** is incorrect because create_registered_model() creates an empty registered model entry in the registry without associating any run artifact or version.

**D** is incorrect because save_model() saves the model to a local path and has no model registry interaction.

[Azure Machine Learning model registry — mlflow.register_model()](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-manage-models-mlflow)
questions.reference: [Manage models registry with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-manage-models-mlflow)

questions.stem: A data scientist loads a model from the Azure Machine Learning model registry using the URI `models:/FraudDetector@champion`. What does `champion` represent?

options.text: The Azure subscription name that contains the model
A model alias that points to a specific registered version
The experiment name that trained the model
The deployment endpoint that currently serves the model

questions.explanation: **B** is correct because in the modern Azure Machine Learning registry workflow, an identifier such as `champion` is commonly used as a **model alias** that resolves to a specific version. **A**, **C**, and **D** are incorrect because the alias is not a subscription, experiment, or endpoint name.
questions.reference: [Manage models, registries, and aliases in Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-manage-models)

questions.stem: An ML engineer wants to load a registered model and run predictions using a generic Python interface that works regardless of the original training framework. Which loading function should they use?

options.text: mlflow.sklearn.load_model('models:/MyModel/1')
mlflow.tensorflow.load_model('models:/MyModel/1')
mlflow.pyfunc.load_model('models:/MyModel/1')
mlflow.load_model('models:/MyModel/1')

questions.explanation:
**C** is correct because mlflow.pyfunc.load_model() loads any MLflow model using the generic Python function flavor, exposing a .predict() method regardless of the original framework (sklearn, XGBoost, TensorFlow, etc.). This makes downstream code framework-agnostic.

**A** is incorrect because mlflow.sklearn.load_model() is a framework-specific loader that only works for sklearn models and returns a native sklearn object.

**B** is incorrect because mlflow.tensorflow.load_model() is a framework-specific loader that only works for TensorFlow models.

**D** is incorrect because mlflow.load_model() is not a valid top-level MLflow function — pyfunc.load_model() is the correct generic interface.

[MLflow flavors — mlflow.pyfunc.load_model()](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-deploy-mlflow-models)
questions.reference: [Guidelines for Deploying MLflow Models - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-deploy-mlflow-models)

questions.stem: A team migrates from the classic MLflow model registry (Staging/Production stages) to the newer Azure ML registry with model aliases. What is the correct way to load the model version tagged as the champion model using aliases?

options.text: mlflow.pyfunc.load_model('models:/MyModel/Production')
mlflow.pyfunc.load_model('models:/MyModel@champion')
MlflowClient().get_model_version_by_alias('MyModel', 'champion')
mlflow.pyfunc.load_model('models:/MyModel/champion')

questions.explanation:
**B** is correct because the newer model alias feature uses the @ syntax to reference a named alias: models:/ModelName@alias. This is the correct format for loading a model version by alias in the Azure ML model registry.

**A** is incorrect because 'Production' is a stage from the classic registry, not an alias — in the newer alias-based approach, you use @ syntax instead.

**C** is incorrect because get_model_version_by_alias() returns version metadata but does not load the model for inference — a separate load_model() call is still needed.

**D** is incorrect because models:/MyModel/champion uses the slash format which refers to stages, not aliases. Aliases require the @ syntax.

[Azure ML model registry — model aliases](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-manage-models-mlflow)
questions.reference: [Manage models registry with MLflow - Azure Machine Learning](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/machine-learning/how-to-manage-models-mlflow)
